coredata.dc:title: Turn-on fluorescent inner filter effect-based B,S,N co-doped carbon quantum dots and vanadium oxide nanoribbons for α-glucosidase activity detection
coredata.dc:creator: Yin, Chenhui
Wu, Meng
Liu, Ting
Fu, Lixing
Sun, Qijun
Chen, Ligang
Niu, Na
coredata.dc:description: A sensitive and effective strategy for the screening of α-glucosidase and its inhibitors was established based on carbon quantum dots (CQDs) and vanadium oxide nanoribbons. B,S,N co-doped CQDs (BSN-CQDs) were prepared by one-step microwave hydrothermal method using o-phenylenediamine, boric acid, and thiourea as raw materials. The prepared CQDs emitted yellow fluorescence (λem = 565 nm) under excitation of 410 nm, and the quantum yield was 15.3%. The fluorescence of CQDs was firstly quenched by the vanadium oxide nanoribbons via inner filter effect. Then, 2-O-α-D-glucopyranosyl-L-ascorbic acid (AA2G) was introduced as a substrate of α-glucosidase to produce ascorbic acid (AA). Vanadium oxide nanoribbons could be converted to V4+ by AA, which led to fluorescence recovery. After adding inhibitors (anti-diabetic drugs), the α-glucosidase activity was inhibited and the degree of fluorescence recovery was reduced. The established nanoprobe showed a response linearity to α-glucosidase in the range of 0.01–5 U mL−1, with a low detection limit of 0.003 U mL−1. This platform was applied for the screening of acarbose, with a detection limit of 0.02 μM. The constructed sensing strategy had the advantages of high selectivity, sensitivity, and was successfully used for α-glucosidase detection in serum samples and anti-diabetic drugs screening.
coredata.dcterms:subject: α-Glucosidase
Turn-on sensing
Fluorescence nanoprobe
Vanadium oxide nanoribbons

xocs:item-toc-entry.xocs:item-toc-section-title: Detection of α-glucosidase in human serum samples
Study on fluorescence recovery process of AA-induced CQDS-V2O5 nanoprobe
Analytical application to human serum samples

xocs:funding.xocs:funding-agency-matched-string: Fundamental Research Funds for the Central Universities

ce:caption.ce:simple-para: Schematic illustration of CQDs synthesis, strategy for α-glucosidase detection based on CQDs-V2O5 nanoprobe.

(A) TEM and HRTEM images, (B) particle size distribution curve, (C) XRD pattern, (D) FT-IR spectra of BSN-CQDs. (E) Fluorescence excitation, emission spectra, and UV–vis spectra of BSN-CQDs (insert: optical images of BSN-CQDs under sunlight on the left and UV light on the right), and (F) emission spectra of BSN-CQDs aqueous solution under various excitation wavelengths.

(A) TEM image of V2O5 nanoribbons, (B) HRTEM image of V2O5 nanoribbons, (C) High-resolution XPS spectrum of V 2p, (D) UV–vis absorption spectrum of V2O5 nanoribbons (blue curve) and bulk V2O5 solution (red curve), (E) FT-IR spectrum of V2O5 nanoribbons, (F) XRD pattern of V2O5 nanoribbons.

(A) Fluorescence spectra of CQDs with various concentrations (0, 10, 20, 30, 40, 50, 60, 70 μg mL−1) of V2O5 nanoribbons. (B) Fluorescence intensity ratio (F0/F) graph of CQDs at 565 nm for V2O5 nanoribbons of different concentrations. (C) Fluorescence excitation, emission spectra of BSN-CQDs and UV–vis spectra of V2O5 nanoribbons, and (D) time-resolved decays of CQDs with and without V2O5 nanoribbons.

(A) Fluorescence emission spectra of BSN-CQDs with different substances. (B) Fluorescence emission spectra of BSN-CQDs with various concentrations of α-glucosidase. (C) Fluorescence response of the constructed sensing platform toward various concentrations of acarbose. (D) Fluorescence response of CQDs-V2O5 nanoprobe/AA2G sensing platform toward various substances.

head.ce:title: Turn-on fluorescent inner filter effect-based B,S,N co-doped carbon quantum dots and vanadium oxide nanoribbons for α-glucosidase activity detection

College of Materials Science and Chemical Engineering

sa:affiliation.sa:organization: College of Chemistry, Chemical Engineering and Resource Utilization
Key Laboratory of Forest Plant Ecology

ce:abstract-sec.ce:simple-para: A sensitive and effective strategy for the screening of α-glucosidase and its inhibitors was established based on carbon quantum dots (CQDs) and vanadium oxide nanoribbons. B,S,N co-doped CQDs (BSN-CQDs) were prepared by one-step microwave hydrothermal method using o-phenylenediamine, boric acid, and thiourea as raw materials. The prepared CQDs emitted yellow fluorescence (λem = 565 nm) under excitation of 410 nm, and the quantum yield was 15.3%. The fluorescence of CQDs was firstly quenched by the vanadium oxide nanoribbons via inner filter effect. Then, 2-O-α-D-glucopyranosyl-L-ascorbic acid (AA2G) was introduced as a substrate of α-glucosidase to produce ascorbic acid (AA). Vanadium oxide nanoribbons could be converted to V4+ by AA, which led to fluorescence recovery. After adding inhibitors (anti-diabetic drugs), the α-glucosidase activity was inhibited and the degree of fluorescence recovery was reduced. The established nanoprobe showed a response linearity to α-glucosidase in the range of 0.01–5 U mL−1, with a low detection limit of 0.003 U mL−1. This platform was applied for the screening of acarbose, with a detection limit of 0.02 μM. The constructed sensing strategy had the advantages of high selectivity, sensitivity, and was successfully used for α-glucosidase detection in serum samples and anti-diabetic drugs screening.

ce:section.ce:para: Diabetes is a common metabolic disorder disease with the feature of high blood glucose level [1]. It is accompanied by serious complications, such as retinopathy, cardiovascular disease, neuropathy, and so on. In all diabetics, patients with non-insulin dependent diabetes (T2D) accounted for more than 90%. Control of postprandial hyperglycemia is critical for the diagnosis and treatment of T2D. α-Glucosidase is a membrane-bound enzyme in the epithelium of the small intestine, which can catalyze the production of α-glucose [2]. It has become one of the most important targets in the treatment of diabetes. α-Glucosidase inhibitors could effectively restrain the activity of α-glucosidase, thereby significantly reduce blood glucose level. In addition, researches show that α-glucosidase inhibitors have broad application prospects in antiviral infection, tumor treatment, and obesity treatment [3]. Therefore, it is crucial to accurately and quantitatively analyze α-glucosidase activity and screening its inhibitors.

So far, plenty of methods for α-glucosidase detection and its inhibitors screening have been prepared. Zhang established a colorimetric method based on gold nanoparticles [4]. Cheng fabricated a colorimetric sensor depending on the etching of gold rods [5]. Li devised a reusable electrochemical sensor using aptamer modified gold nanoparticles [6]. Liu developed a fluorimetric sensor based on β-cyclodextrin-coated quantum dots [7]. Yam devised a fluorimetric sensor on account of a platinum complex-polymer ensemble [8]. However, due to the inherent shortcomings of colorimetric and electrochemical methods, these methods cannot meet the requirements of rapid and sensitive detection. For instance, the drawback of colorimetric sensors is their unsatisfactory sensitivity and selectivity. The electrochemical sensors have inherent problems of poor reproducibility. Fluorimetric method is preferred owing to superiorities of high sensitivity, convenient operation, and time saving. Unfortunately, some probes in these fluorimetric methods are complex to synthesize, expensive, and high toxicity, which burdens their practical application.

To surmount these difficulties, CQDs have become dramatic candidate materials for the construction of fluorescent probes owing to their tunable emission, high light stability, water solubility, excellent biocompatibility, easy preparation and modification [9,10]. However, most of the prepared CQDs emit blue light after being excited, and the autofluorescence of the biological matrix is also blue, which limits the application of CQDs in biological analysis and detection [11]. In contrast, CQDs with longer fluorescence emission are more suitable for biological analysis, but their quantum yield (QY) is low. Studies have shown that doping heteroatoms into CQDs can introduce new defect energy levels and surface morphology, which can also increase the fluorescence QY. Fu prepared nitrogen-doped CQDs with citric acid and ethylenediamine as raw materials, and the QY was increased from 3.7% to 67.4% compared with the undoped CQDs prepared from soybean [12]. Therefore, the application of CQDs in analytical sensing can be broadened by doping heteroatoms. However, fluorescence sensors based on CQDs mainly rely on fluorescence quenching (turn-off) and have low selectivity.

Compared with the turn-off fluorescence sensors, the turn-on fluorescence sensors are less likely to produce false positive signals [13,14]. As transition metal oxide nanomaterials, MnO2 and CoOOH nanosheets can quench the fluorescence intensity of CQDs through surface functional groups or surface adsorption reactions [15,16]. The introduction of CQDs into CoOOH and MnO2 nanomaterials has greatly promoted the development of sensitive turn-on fluorescent sensors. Zhang et al. synthesized CQDs with gelatin as raw material and developed CQDs-CoOOH nanosheets fluorescence turn-on sensor for detecting AA in view of the redox reaction between CoOOH and AA [17]. Tan et al. used citric acid and polyethyleneimine to synthesize CQDs and constructed CQDs-MnO2 fluorescence turn-on nanosensor for detecting dopamine based on the redox reaction of MnO2 and dopamine [18]. Vanadium oxide (V2O5) nanoribbons are novel transition metal oxide nanomaterials with excellent properties such as redox activity and good stability [19]. They have a strong ultraviolet absorption capacity with maximum absorption around 390 nm, indicating good quenchers for fluorescent materials. Therefore, CQDs and V2O5 nanoribbons can be coupled to develop novel fluorescent turn-on nanosensors.

In this study, low-cost and high photoluminescent CQDs were prepared from organic compounds (o-phenylenediamine, boric acid, and thiourea) by microwave hydrothermal method. The doping of boron, sulfur, and nitrogen heteroatoms in CQDs is conducive to improving fluorescence properties. By employing the synthesized CQDs, we have developed a platform based on CQDs-V2O5 fluorescent nanoprobe for α-glucosidase activity detection (shown in Fig. 1 ). The fluorescence of CQDs could be quenched through the V2O5 nanoribbons. When AA was present, the V2O5 nanoribbons were devastated and the fluorescence was restored. Based on this principle, the sensing turn-on strategy was constructed for ultrasensitive detection of α-glucosidase. The constructed sensing strategy manifested such advantages as high selectivity, sensitivity without using expensive aptamers or antibodies.

o-Phenylenediamine, boric acid, thiourea, ammonium metavanadate, hydrogen peroxide aqueous solution (H2O2, 30 wt%) were purchased from Macklin (Shanghai, China). α-glucosidase (α-glu), lysozyme (LYZ), alkaline phosphatase (ALP), acid phosphatase (ACP), catalase (CAT), trypsin (TRY), β-glucosidase (β-glu), glucose, uric acid, glutathione (GSH), alanine (Ala), cysteine (Cys), tyrosine (Tyr), leucine (Leu), arginine (Arg), and metal chloride were obtained from Aladdin (Shanghai, China). Acarbose was obtained from Rhawn (Shanghai, China). The human serum was supplied from the Hospital of Northeast Forestry University. Ultrapure water was provided by a Milli-Q water system (Millipore, Billerica, MA, USA).

The UV–vis spectra were recorded by a Perkin-Elmer spectrophotometer. The fluorescence spectra were taken on an F-4600 fluorescence spectrophotometer (Hitachi, Japan) with the exciting and emission slits of 10 nm, respectively. Fourier transform infrared spectra (FT-IR) was conducted on a spectrum 400 spectrometer (Perkin Elmer, USA). X-ray diffraction (XRD) spectra were collected on a TD-3500 spectrometer (Tongda, China). Fluorescence lifetime was performed with single photon counting QuantaMaster 8000 Modular Fluorescence Spectrometer (Horiba, Canada). CQDs were prepared by MDS-15 microwave digestion workstation (Sineo, China). The size and morphology distributions of CQDs and V2O5 nanoribbons in aqueous solution were investigated by transmission electron microscope (TEM) on JEM-2100 (JEOL, Japan).

BSN-CQDs were synthesized by one-pot microwave hydrothermal method. In brief, 50 mg o-phenylenediamine, 60 mg boric acid, 60 mg thiourea were mixed with 10 mL ultrapure water and sonicated for 10 min, then the mixture was transferred to a wave digestion tank and maintained at 160 °C for 20 min. The color of the solution changed from colorless to yellow–brown. The resulting solution was filtered through a microporous membrane and dialyzed through a dialysis bag (3500 Da). Finally, CQDs powder was obtained by rotary evaporation and freeze-drying dialysate.

V2O5 nanoribbons were prepared based on the previous method [20]. At first, bulk V2O5 powder was synthesized by heating ammonium metavanadate in air atmosphere at 400 °C for 2 h. Then, 100 mg of procured bulk V2O5 powder was added to 30 mL of ultrapure water and sonicated for 5 min to form a yellow suspension. After that, 3 mL of 30 wt% H2O2 aqueous solution was added dropwise to the suspension and stirred for 5 min to form an orange-yellow solution. Subsequently, the obtained orange-yellow solution was transferred to a 50 mL autoclave and maintained at 180 °C for 24 h. Finally, the V2O5 nanoribbons powder was obtained by freeze-drying solution.

CQDs-V2O5 nanoprobe was fabricated in following steps: 20 μL CQDs solution and 20 μL V2O5 nanoribbons solution (60 μg mL−1) were mixed with PBS buffer (pH = 6.8, 50 mM) and diluted to a total volume of 100 μL. After incubating for 3 min, the CQDs-V2O5 nanoprobe was obtained.

First, 250 μL of α-glucosidase solution of various concentrations and 25 μL substrate AA2G (2.0 mM) solution were incubated in 125 μL of PBS buffer solution (pH = 6.8, 50 mM) at 37 °C for 30 min. Subsequently, 100 μL of the fabricated CQDs-V2O5 nanoprobe solution was added into mixture. After incubating at room temperature for 4 min, the fluorescence spectra and the fluorescence intensity at 565 nm were recorded under excitation at 410 nm.

As a classical α-glucosidase inhibitor, acarbose was used to prove the feasibility of the constructed sensing platform. Then, 250 μL α-glucosidase solution, 25 μL substrate AA2G solution, and 50 μL various concentrations of acarbose were with 50 μL PBS buffer solution (pH = 6.8, 50 mM) at 37 °C for 30 min. Subsequently, 100 μL of the fabricated CQDs-V2O5 nanoprobe solution was added into mixture. After incubating at room temperature for 4 min, the fluorescence spectra and the fluorescence intensity at 565 nm were recorded under excitation at 410 nm.

ce:section.ce:section-title: Detection of α-glucosidase in human serum samples
ce:section.ce:para: The capacity of sensing platforms for α-glucosidase in human serum samples was studied. Different concentrations of α-glucosidase were spiked into human serum samples (containing 0.2 mM N-ethylmaleimide) and diluted 10 times with PBS buffer (pH = 6.8, 50 mM) to prepare spiked samples. The spiked samples were tested with the aforesaid α-glucosidase detection process.

o-Phenylenediamine contains the aromatic structure and amino group, which is an ideal precursor for constructing long-wavelength CQDs. Lv et al. synthesized yellow-emissive CQDs with o-phenylenediamine as carbon source [21]. However, the QY of o-phenylenediamine derived CQDs without heteroatomic doping was only 3.5% [21], which was lower than that in the present work (15.3%). To overcome this shortage, heteroatom doping had been adopted to prepare o-phenylenediamine derived CQDs. In the current work, we discussed the influence of different dopants on fluorescence (Fig. S1A-D). When single doped with B or S, the heteroatoms could enhance the fluorescence intensity, but the effect was not obvious. However, the fluorescence intensity was significantly enhanced when B and S were co-doped. The results showed that the fluorescence intensity was the highest when the co-doping mass ratio was 1:1. Therefore, we chose 50 mg o-phenylenediamine, 60 mg boric acid, and 60 mg thiourea to synthesize BSN-CQDs. As for the synthesis method, the microwave hydrothermal method was adopted to synthesize CQDs because of its inherent convenience, high efficiency, and environmental protection. In order to obtain the highest QY of BSN-CQDs, the reaction conditions including the reaction time and temperature. The influence of reaction time was investigated and the results were shown in Fig. S1E and results showed that 20 min was the optimal reaction time for BSN-CQDs. The effect of reaction time was also studied and the results showed that 160 °C was the optimal temperature (Fig. S1F). Under optimized synthesis conditions, the relative QY of BSN-CQDs was 15.3% with rhodamine 6G as the reference. The result is higher than most reported yellow-emissive CQDs [22,23].

To confirm the particle morphology and size analysis of BSN-CQDs, TEM of BSN-CQDs were surveyed. As presented in the TEM image (Fig. 2 A), the BSN-CQDs display monodisperse and spherical distribution. Moreover, the lattice structure can be obviously observed from the HRTEM image (inset in Fig. 2A) with a lattice spacing of 0.21 nm, which matches well with the graphene (100) crystal plane [24]. The BSN-CQDs display a size distribution range of 1.4–4.6 nm and the average diameter of BSN-CQDs is 3.0 nm (Fig. 2B).
The XRD analysis was investigated and the broad diffraction peak of the XRD image (Fig. 2C) at 21.1° matched C (002) plane. Results indicated the amorphous property of CQDs [25].

The FT-IR analysis (Fig. 2D) was employed to validate the functional groups of BSN-CQDs. The wide absorption peak at 3414 cm−1 is assigned to –OH/–NH2 stretching vibration. The strong absorption peak at 1615 cm−1 is observed, which is ascribed to the vibration of C = C/C = O. The absorption peak at 1457 cm−1 corresponds to the vibration of B-N. The distinct absorption peak at 1397 cm−1 is attributed to the stretching vibration of C-N [26]. The absorption peak located at 1072 cm−1 is from the stretching vibrations of C-S/C-B [27]. In addition, XPS analysis of BSN-CQD was implemented, and the results were shown in Supplemental Information (Fig. S2). In conclusion, it can be inferred that B, S, and N are successfully doped to the surface of BSN-CQDs due to the condensation reaction among the precursors.

Fluorescence excitation, emission spectra, and UV–vis spectra were recorded to investigate the optical features of BSN-CQDs. There are two obvious absorption bands located at 276 nm and 410 nm (Fig. 2E). The band at 276 nm is ascribed to π-π* transition of the aromatic sp2 structure. The band at 410 nm is probably from n-π* transition of C = O, as a result of bright luminescence. Compared with other CQDs, the absorption bands have a redshift, indicating a lower transition from excited state to ground state [28]. Consequently, the as-prepared BSN-CQDs display considerably long-wavelength optimal emission at 565 nm with optimal excitation at 410 nm (Fig. 2E). Noteworthily, excitation-independence is observed, probably due to their homogeneous size and surface state (Fig. 2F) [29]. In addition, the effect of various pH values towards fluorescence was studied. The fluorescence continuously enhances accompanied by an increase of pH values from 2.0 to 6.0 and remains stable at 6.0–10.0, which could attribute to photoinduced electron transfer process (Fig. S3) [30]. The photobleaching resistance and salt tolerance of BSN-CQDs have also been explored. After 1.5 h of UV irradiation, the fluorescence intensity of the BSN-CQDs hardly changed, which proves that BSN-CQDs have strong photo-stability (Fig. S4A). In a larger concentration of NaCl solution, the fluorescence intensity of the BSN-CQDs remained stable, proving that the BSN-CQDs have strong salt tolerance (Fig. S4B). In summary, such features provided possibilities for new CQDs to fabricate biosensing nanoprobes.

V2O5 nanoribbons were synthesized by hydrothermal method and characterized in detail. The TEM image (Fig. 3 A) reveals that the morphology of the V2O5 nanoribbons is an obvious ribbon structure with a width of 20–100 nm. In addition, the lattice structure spacing of 0.35 and 0.44 nm is observed in the HRTEM image (Fig. 3B), corresponding to the (110) and (200) planes [31,32], respectively. As shown in Fig. S5A, three distinctive peaks at 517.4, 530.0, 631.3 eV are assigned to V 2p, O 1 s, and V 2 s. The O 1 s spectra (Fig. S5B) include two distinct peaks at 530.5 (V-O) and 531.6 eV (V-OH) and the V 2p spectra (Fig. 3C) include two characteristic peaks at 517.7 (V5+ 2p3/2) and 525.0 eV (V5+ 2p1/2). The strong absorption peak around 390 nm is ascribed to electronic transition between O 2p and V 3d orbitals (vibration of V = O), which is significantly different from that of bulk V2O5 (Fig. 3D) [33]. The functional groups of V2O5 nanoribbons are further detected with FI-IR analysis (Fig. 3E). The distinct absorption peak at 3130 and 1608 cm−1 is ascribed to the vibration of –OH. The absorption peak at 923 cm−1 corresponds to vibration of V = O, which is consistent with the UV–vis results. The absorption peaks located at 839 cm−1 and 539 cm−1 are from the vibrations of V-O-V and V-O [34]. The absorption peak at 923 cm-1 corresponds to the vibration of V-OH2 from the influence of water. The absorption peak of V2O5 nanoribbons in the XRD image (Fig. 3F) differs from that of bulk V2O5 distinctly, indicating that the structure of the nanoribbons has changed after hydrothermal synthesis.

The fluorescence intensity of BSN-CQDs can be significantly quenched by the V2O5 nanoribbons as demonstrated in Fig. 4 A. When V2O5 nanoribbons concentrations were increased from 0 to 60 μg mL−1, the fluorescence intensity of CQDs at 565 nm abruptly decreased. When V2O5 nanoribbons concentration was higher than 60 μg mL−1, the fluorescence intensity was slightly quenched. Therefore, 60 μg mL−1 of V2O5 nanoribbons was chosen as the optimal concentration. To investigate the quenching efficiency, the relationship between the ratio F0/F and the concentrations of V2O5 nanoribbons was investigated according to Stern-Volmer equation F0/F = Kq·[Q] + 1, where F or F0 represented the fluorescence intensity with or without V2O5 nanoribbons, Kq and [C] standard for quenching constant and concentration. As seen in Fig. 4B, V2O5 nanoribbons have a good quenching effect on BSN-CQDs.

To better elucidate the generation of quenching effect, we first studied the fluorescence spectrum of CQDs and the absorption spectrum of the V2O5 nanoribbons since spectral overlap is required for FRET and IFE to occur in widely reported fluorescence quenching mechanisms. IFE results from the absorption of excitation and/or emission light by at least one absorber in the sensing platform, whereas FRET process is associated with dipole–dipole interactions between donor–acceptor pairs [35,39,40]. The absorption peak of V2O5 nanoribbons was around 390 nm, while the maximum excitation and emission wavelengths of CQDs are 410 nm and 565 nm, respectively (Fig. 4C). V2O5 nanoribbons acted as a filter by partially absorbing excitation radiation, described as primary IFE. In this sensing platform, CQDs are fluorophores, V2O5 nanoribbons are absorbers and α-glucosidases are the analytes. In this case, the fluorescence of CQDs decreased due to IFE. However, V2O5 nanoribbons was destroyed in the presence of ascorbic acid, and the fluorescence of CQDs was recovered due to the inhibition of IFE. The α-glucosidase activity can generate ascorbic acid by hydrolyzing the substrate AA2G. Therefore, the α-glucosidase activity can be analyzed by the degree of CQDs fluorescence recovery. There was a huge overlap between the absorption spectrum of the absorber (V2O5 nanoribbons) and the excitation spectrum of the fluorophore (CQDs), suggesting that an inner filter effect (IFE) may have occurred. FRET requires that the emission spectrum of CQDs overlap with the absorption spectrum of the quencher [35]. As shown in Fig. 4C, there was almost no overlap between the emission spectrum of CQDs and the absorption spectrum of the V2O5 nanoribbons, indicating that FRET did not occur. Afterward, the Zeta potential of nanomaterials in PBS buffer solution was measured (pH = 6.8, 50 mM). The zeta potential of CQDs and V2O5 nanoribbons were −9.5 and −6.9 mV, respectively. Both nanomaterials had the same negative charge, indicating that there was no electrostatic interaction between them. Furthermore, the TEM image of fabricated CQDs-V2O5 nanoprobe was investigated (Fig. S6A). No CQDs adsorbed on the surface of V2O5 nanoribbons were found, implying that there was no combination to form a complex. The inference could also be proved from the UV absorption spectrum before and after the construction of the CQDs-V2O5 nanoprobe. As demonstrated in Fig. S7A, each retained inherent absorption peaks in the mixture, implying that no direct reaction took place between V2O5 nanoribbons and CQDs. The static quenching process could therefore be ruled out. It was essential that the fluorescence lifetime of CQDs was examined before (1.89 ns) and after (1.71 ns) quenching and changes were negligible (Fig. 4D), which complied with the premise of the IFE process. Dynamic quenching and FRET were also excluded, because they were accompanied by changes in fluorescence lifetime of CQDs [35]. In summary, it is most likely that IFE plays a dominant role in quenching process.

ce:section.ce:section-title: Study on fluorescence recovery process of AA-induced CQDS-V2O5 nanoprobe
ce:section.ce:para: In order to study the fluorescence recovery process of the CQDs-V2O5 nanoprobe induced by AA, the UV–vis absorption spectra were recorded. As shown in Fig. S7A, there is an absorption peak at 390 nm of the CQDs-V2O5 nanoprobe, which is attributed to the vibration of V = O. The absorption peak at 390 nm is significantly reduced with the addition of AA because V2O5 can be incompletely converted to V4+ by AA based on redox reaction. The high-resolution spectra of V 2p were conducted to verify the details of chemical bonding changes. As demonstrated in Fig. S7B, four dominant peaks at 516.3, 517.4, 523.8, and 525.0 eV are revealed, ascribed to V4+ 2p3/2, V5+ 2p3/2, V4+ 2p1/2, and V5+ 2p1/2, respectively. TEM image (Fig. S6B) confirms that the V2O5 nanoribbons structure is completely degraded by AA. As a result, the IFE-induced quenching process is obstructed and the fluorescence is restored.

We examined the sensitivity of the CQDs-V2O5 nanoprobe to AA because the sensing strategy for the screening of α-glucosidase and its inhibitors was related to the production of AA. As exhibited in Fig. S8A, the CQDs-V2O5 nanoprobe shows a good fluorescence response with the increasing AA concentration. More importantly, there is a linear equation of F/F0 = 0.00284[CAA] + 1.05 (R2 = 0.994) with AA concentrations in the range of 1 to 500 μM (Fig. S8B). The detection limit (LOD) is calculated as 0.31 μM by three times the ratio of blank measurement standard deviation to linear equation slope. It shows that the probe has a high sensitivity to AA, which provides the possibility for trace detection of α-glucosidase.

The factors affecting the fluorescence recovery process were further investigated. Since AA played a decisive role in the fluorescence recovery process, we optimized the corresponding reaction time and pH value. The optimization result of V2O5 nanoribbons was seen in Fig. 4A and the optimal concentration was 60 μg mL−1. The fluorescence intensity gradually increased and reached a plateau in 4 min, revealing the rapid degradation of V2O5 nanoribbons through AA (Fig. S9A). The fluorescence recovery degree of the CQDs-V2O5 nanoprobe reached a maximum at pH = 6.8 (Fig. S9B). Therefore, 4 min and 6.8 were considered as optimized reaction time and pH values. The behavior of enzymatic hydrolysis of substrates has also been investigated. The process by which α-glucosidase catalyzed the release of AA from substrate AA2G had also been investigated (Fig. S9C). When the concentration was 2.0 mM, the highest degree of fluorescence recovery was obtained, which was regarded as the optimal substrate concentration. Whereafter, PBS buffers with various pH values were constructed (Fig. S9D), and α-glucosidase displayed the strongest activity at pH = 6.8. Afterward, the fluorescence intensity was enhanced continuously in 30 min and held steady after that (Fig. S9E). In addition, the influence of incubation temperature (Fig. S9F) on the fluorescence recovery process was studied under pH = 6.8 and the optimal incubation temperature was 37 °C.

The feasibility of developed strategy for α-glucosidase detection and inhibitors screening was studied by investigating the influence of different substrates on CQDs fluorescence. AA2G, α-glucosidase, and inhibitors show negligible effects on CQDs (in Fig. 5 A), and only when AA2G and α-glucosidase are mixed together will the fluorescence of CQDs-V2O5 nanoprobe recover. In addition, the reproducibility of the sensor has been investigated (Fig. S11). For five consecutive measurements of different CQDs-V2O5 nanoprobe/AA2G, the RSD of the fluorescence response to 0.5 U mL−1 (Fig. S11A) and 5 U mL−1 (Fig. S11B) α-glucosidase was 2.6% and 3.2%, respectively. The results show that the novel sensors have good reproducibility.

Under optimized conditions (details in Fig. S9 of Supplemental Information), the fluorescence emission spectra of CQDs were investigated. Noticeably, the fluorescence spectra of the CQDs-V2O5 nanoprobe gradually recovers as the α-glucosidase concentration increases (Fig. 5B). Fig. S10A reveals that the fluorescence ratio F/F0 increases in direct proportion with the concentration of α-glucosidase. A good linear equation is expressed as F/F0 = 0.186[Cα-glu] + 1.06 (R2 = 0.994) and its linear range is 0.01 U mL−1 to 5 U mL−1. The LOD is computed to be 0.003 U mL−1. The relative standard deviation (RSD) of repeated assessments (n = 11) is 3.7%.

Effective selectivity is essential for the practical application of sensing platforms. Different interfering substances are added to the detection system including 1000 μM metal ions, 1000 μM negative ions, 1000 μM small biomolecules, and 10 U mL−1 biomacromolecules. Fig. 5D shows that the effects of most substances on the fluorescence of CQDs-V2O5 nanoprobe /AA2G are negligible, glutathione and cysteine have little effect, and only α-glucosidase has obvious fluorescence recovery effects on them. These results indicate that the fluorescent platform based on CQDs-V2O5 nanoprobe /AA2G is selective for α-glucosidase.

The established fluorescence sensing platform is used to screen α-glucosidase inhibitors and reveal the inhibitory ability by investigating fluorescence recovery. The inhibition ratio (I, %), expressed as an equation I (%) = (F-F0)/(Fq-F0) × 100, was usually employed to perform the inhibiting ability. Fq stands for the fluorescence intensity of CQDs-V2O5 nanoprobe/AA2G without α-glucosidase, F0 stands for the fluorescence intensity of CQDs-V2O5 nanoprobe/AA2G with α-glucosidase (5 U mL−1), and F stands for CQDs-V2O5 nanoprobe/AA2G/α-glucosidase with α-glucosidase inhibitors. Acarbose, a well-known α-glucosidase inhibitor, was used to determine the feasibility of employing the platform to screen α-glucosidase inhibitors. As the concentration of acarbose increases, the activity of α-glucosidase is inhibited. The process reduces the production of AA, resulting in a weaker fluorescence recovery than when the corresponding inhibitor is not added. Fig. 5C reveals fluorescence quenched by increasing acarbose concentration. The regression equation is I = 16.2log[Cacarbose] + 20.4 with acarbose concentration from 0.1 μM to 1000 μM and IC50 is 63.1 μM (Fig. S10B). The LOD is computed to be 0.02 μM, indicating that the platform has an ultra-sensitive response to acarbose [36].

ce:section.ce:section-title: Analytical application to human serum samples
ce:section.ce:para: The capacity of proposed fluorescence sensing platform for α-glucosidase in real samples was surveyed. N-Ethylmaleimide (0.2 mM) was added to eliminate the interference of Cys and GSH that might exist in the serum [37]. Three human serum samples were analyzed and the results were shown in Table S1. The results of unspiked samples measured by proposed method were 0.032, 0.048, and 0.041 U mL−1, respectively. The average recovery of α-glucosidase is 97.2%-106.0%, and the RSD is less than 4.6%. Furthermore, to evaluate the accuracy of the proposed method, the detection results of this method were compared with those of the traditional colorimetric method [38]. As shown in Table S1, the results of the traditional colorimetric method were 0.034, 0.047 and 0.045 U mL−1 in unspiked samples, which proves that the detection results of the established method are consistent with the results of the colorimetric method. However, the sensing mechanism established by traditional colorimetric methods relies on the chromogenic substrate (p-nitrophenol) with a low molar absorption coefficient, resulting in insufficient sensitivity. In this paper, the proposed fluorescence method can further optimize the scheme by improving the QY of fluorescent probes (CQDs), making the results more sensitive. The above results indicate that the biological matrix has negligible influence on the ability to α-glucosidase detection and turn-on fluorescence CQDs-V2O5 nanoprobe can be applied to detect α-glucosidase in real samples.

The method in this work has been compared with other methods for α-glucosidase detection as well (Table S2). This method has disadvantages such as slightly higher LOD than several methods. However, its linear range is wide. Thanks to their unique advantages such as rapidness, simplicity, and low-cost, fluorescent probes could be used more widely. In addition, the turn-on fluorescent strategy makes detection α-glucosidase more reliable.

Novel BSN-CQDs have been developed with high quantum yield and yellow photoluminescence via one-pot microwave hydrothermal synthesis. The IFE-induced quenching process, enzyme-specific hydrolysis, and AA-based reduction reaction was cleverly combined. A turn-on fluorescent strategy for α-glucosidase ultrasensitive detection was established based on an as-fabricated CQDs-V2O5 nanoprobe. Subsequently, the facile and low-cost fluorescence nanoprobe was successfully applied to detect α-glucosidase and its inhibitors. Therefore, the CQDs-V2O5 nanoprobe based strategy was a fast, sensitive and inexpensive method to detect α-glucosidase and proved its potential application in clinical diagnosis and anti-diabetic drugs discovery.

Chenhui Yin: Conceptualization, Methodology, Writing – review & editing. Meng Wu: Data curation, Investigation. Ting Liu: Investigation. Lixing Fu: Conceptualization. Qijun Sun: Methodology. Ligang Chen: Funding acquisition, Writing – review & editing, Supervision. Na Niu: Funding acquisition, Writing – review & editing, Supervision.

ce:conflict-of-interest.ce:para: The authors declare that they have no known competing financial interests or personal relationships that could have appeared to influence the work reported in this paper.

ce:acknowledgment.ce:para: This project was funded by the Fundamental Research Funds for the Central Universities (2572021AW42), 111 Project (B20088), Heilongjiang Touyan Innovation Team Program (Tree Genetics and Breeding Innovation Team) and Key Laboratory of Superlight Materials and Surface Technology (Harbin Engineering University).
